original_abstract:
This paper details the outcomes of the ParlaMint II project, which provides a comprehensive collection of comparable corpora featuring parliamentary debates from 29 European countries and autonomous regions. Containing over 1 billion words and covering the period from 2015 to at least 2022, the corpora are uniformly encoded and enriched with extensive metadata for more than 24,000 speakers. The data is linguistically processed up to the level of Universal Dependencies syntax and named entities. 

We focus on the significant infrastructure and content enhancements implemented since the project's first phase. We describe our collaborative compilation process, which utilized GitHub for version control and validation, a centralized pipeline for distribution, and CLARIN services for long-term dissemination. Beyond a quantitative overview of the data, the paper highlights qualitative advancements: localized and expanded metadata (including political party orientations), the creation of machine-translated English versions of the corpora tagged with semantic classes, and the development of pilot speech-to-text datasets. We conclude by discussing outreach initiatives, such as shared tasks and hackathons, alongside future directions for expanding the scope and depth of parliamentary data resources.
humanized_abstract:
The paper describes the outcome of the ParlaMint II project that produces a collection of multilingual matched corpora of parliamentary speeches from 29 European countries and regions. Our resources contain approximately 1 billion words and cover 7 years of parliamentary proceedings of 24 institutions in 29 different language varieties from 2015 to at least 2022, matched and accompanied by rich metadata for over 24,000 individual politicians. The speech material has been linguistically processed up to the level of Universal Dependencies syntax annotation and named entities.

This paper focuses on the latter part of the project focusing on the newly added infrastructure and resources. We shortly describe our collaborative effort for compiling the materials for annotation, which mainly involved the use of GitHub for repository validation, version control and the centralised dissemination pipeline and also making use of CLARIN resources. Apart from the quantitative view of the provided data, we provide also some qualitative information about the newly available metadata (local and national) and about the enriched versions of some of the corpora, such as their fully machine translated to English versions, with semantically tagged documents, as well as about the new pilot speech to text sets. Finally we give some information about our outreach activities such as the shared tasks and the hackathons, and plan for possible future developments and expansions of the parliamentary data resources we provided.
